# Transcrição: 11-What Should You Share

- **Fonte:** 11-What Should You Share
- **Duração:** 05:55
- **Idioma:** en
- **Modelo:** small

---

## [00:00 - 03:00]

This is sad but true. One of the most useful exercises I use with my corporate clients is I go through an inventory of all of the things that might be interesting to other people. And they're often astounded by this list, things that have never occurred to them. I'm including a PDF now. It's the same PDF I use with my clients. All the things that you should be thinking about talking about as part of stories and just that subtle art of sharing. But there's another step I want you to take after you're done. So for example, one of the things we should always share is if we're parents, share that we're parents. But I also want you to take that to the next level, to as many levels as you can. So I am a parent. I am a parent of two children. My children are currently nine and thirteen. I have a boy and I have a girl. My daughter has autism. My son loves Minecraft. My son plays Little League baseball. My daughter is a member of an organization that promotes LGBTQ rights. Both of my children are Jewish. I am not Jewish. My children don't eat any foods whatsoever except foods that are white and gray. All of those things fit into the category of my children. And can you see how me being a parent might connect to somebody, but me being a parent of children who only eat pasta and apples might connect with a parent? The fact that my daughter has autism may connect with someone. The fact that my son plays Little League might connect to someone. What we need to do is take that thing, parenthood, that we think people will find relatable, emotionally connecting, and take it as far as we can. I'm a Yankees fan, which means I'm a baseball fan. I'm a Yankees fan who grew up in Massachusetts. I'm a Yankees fan who grew up in Massachusetts because his stepfather was a terrible person and also a Red Sox fan. So out of spite, I became a Yankees fan. I'm a Yankees fan who attended two perfect games in his life, David Wells and David Cohn. I am a Yankees fan who wept in 1996 when they won the World Series. All of those things and more are the levels that I occupy as a Yankees fan. And all of them might be relevant to both a Red Sox fan, a Yankees fan, a baseball fan, someone who has a stepfather who's particularly awful. When we take the things that we think make us interesting, unique, relatable, connectable, emotionally connective, and we go through the levels that they contain, we can often

## [03:00 - 05:55]

find new and interesting things that people might be interested in. So that when I'm being silent in a room and I'm inventorying information and I find out that someone's a baseball fan and I find out they're a Red Sox fan, well now I know, I'm going to mention I'm a Yankees fan. But because they're a Red Sox fan, I'm going to also mention that I grew up in Massachusetts. And I'm also going to mention that I'm only a Yankees fan because of my stepfather. And then I'm going to mention other things like how I think the Red Sox have actually lost their identity by winning the World Series and that it was actually better for Red Sox fans when they hadn't won the World Series. All of those things I will use because I know the person sitting across from me is a Rabbit Red Sox fan. I'll take it as far as I can go with as many layers as I can go to make the deepest connections I can make. So I want you to do the inventory that I've provided here, but then I want you to take each one of those inventory items and think about what else can I say about them? What else makes them special? What other things about my inventory items make me unique or different? What other ways can I connect to other people using them? This is going to be the way that you're able to go into the world and share of yourself so that when you pass through a party, when you sit at a dinner, when you're presenting in a boardroom, when you're standing on a stage, people are not only going to remember the content that you are providing, but the person who is speaking the content that is more important than anything else. There's always going to be another product. There's always going to be another marketing pitch. There's always going to be another advertising campaign, but there's one of you. Do you want them remembering the marketing plan before the other marketing plan, before the other marketing plan? Or do you want them remembering that guy who said those things that I remember and I want to hear again? Or that lady who is standing on the stage talking about some service her company was offering, what was very compelling and I felt connected to while she was doing it. That's what you want to be. Don't be your content. Be yourself. Share of yourself. Be nuanced. Be strategic. Listen. Gather information. Play the interests of the people around you and then share of yourself. Become the person that people remember. Become the person that they want to spend more time with. Become the person that they feel connected to so that the things you say, the things you believe, the things that you want them to believe will be more true for them because they'll believe in you.
